text: This video demonstrates the combined procedure of laparoscopic and intraspector resection for low rectal cancer. This procedure is pioneered by Eric Rollier, professor of surgery in Bordeaux. He divided the low rectal cancer into four types depending on the distance from the dentate line. This young lady has a type two anterior tumour. shown here on the MRI to be two centimeters from the dentate line. The tumor was downstaged by long course chemoradiotherapy.

The surgeon uses five laparoscopic ports to access the abdominal cavity. Dissection begins at the inferior mesenteric vein, proceeding laterally to expose the ureter near the spleen and splenic flexure. The dissection continues at the inferior mesenteric artery, which is skeletonized and controlled with haemlocks. The mesenteric vein is controlled and divided. The sigmoid colon is mobilized from lateral to medial using blunt and sharp dissection, employing an energy device with a monopolar tip and bipolar ligature technology.

The surgeon continues lateral to medial dissection to mobilize the splenic flexure. Subsequently, the rectum is mobilized within the total mesorectal excision (TME) planes, using adequate traction and counter-traction through the left iliac fossa and suprapubic ports.

The surgeon divides the peritoneal reflection anterior to the rectum and continues the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection down to the pelvic floor. Laparoscopic dissection progresses into the intersphincteric plane by dividing the rectococcygeal fibers from the abdominal side. A Lone Star retractor is used to facilitate transanal dissection, and the rectum is divided above the dentate line, completing the resection in the intersphincteric plane while communicating with the abdominal approach.

The surgeon extracts the rectum transanally and divides the specimen at the proximal resection margin. The prepared colon is sutured to the dentate line using a hand-sewn coloanal anastomosis, ensuring proper tension and blood supply. A tube drain is inserted through the left iliac fossa and placed in the pelvis. A defunctioning loop ileostomy is performed in the right iliac fossa, followed by closure of the surgical wounds.
